text: The purpose of this paper is to present a summary of the analysis of the application of the so-called Act on Decomunization carried out after several years of its entry into force, as well as the Supreme Administrative Court’s judgements presented in this regard. The main problem underlying the subject matter of the article can be reduced to the question of what range of tasks should be implemented by the voivodes when investigating the facts that should serve as the basis for conducting proceedings under the Act on Decomunization. This question should be clarified, taking into account the opinions of the Institute of National Remembrance (Instytut Pamięci Narodowej, IPN) issued in relation to these proceedings. As it has been shown in the analyses published in this paper, the courts’ judgements, which — in line with the views on the matter introduced by the Supreme Administrative Court — have a significant impact on the practice of law in relation to decomunization proceedings, suggest that the voivodes are not bound by the IPN’s opinions. One of the assumptions of this paper is that the IPN’s opinions should be treated as part of the evidence that needs to be verified, and which — under the Act on Decomunization — constitutes a basis for conducting proceedings in each case. In other words, it should be verified every time, at the request of the voivode. This approach was presented with a condition that — conceptually speaking — the verification of the IPN’s opinions by the voivodes should not refer to any aspects of the substance of these proceedings, but only to their methodological aspects. In practice, however, it has been shown in the analyses included in this publication that verification of the IPN’s opinions that go beyond methodological issues is possible. The conclusions contained in this paper are presented against the backdrop of the author’s belief in the following: — a verification of the IPN’s opinions contained in this Act requires an analysis of the wording of the Act itself, which leads to the conclusion that the author of the Act assumed the IPN’s opinions to be binding, including in relation to the activities implemented by the voivodes; — the IPNIP’s opinions should not be treated as ordinary part of the evidence, therefore they do not require verification of their correctness by the voivodes; an interpretation of the IPN’s opinions as statements of knowledge by a state authority cooperating with the voivodes in the implementation of activities and proceedings under the Act on Decomunization appears to be most fitting from a systemic perspective.
